title: MCDA Value Function Types
text: Value functions mathematical transformations convert raw clinical outcome values standardized scale (typically 0-100) representing “value” “desirability” decision-making perspective. choice value function reflects stakeholder preferences outcomes valued.

title: MCDA Value Function Types
text: Consider adverse event rate: - Linear: Going 10% 20% AE rate negative value 20% 30% - Exponential: Going 10% 20% concerning, 20% 30% dramatically worse - Threshold: 15% acceptable (value=100), 15% unacceptable (value=0) choice affects benefit-risk conclusions reflect clinical reality stakeholder preferences.

title: MCDA Value Function Types
text: Description: Constant marginal value - unit change equal importance Increasing direction (higher better, e.g., efficacy): v(x)=100×x−minmax−minv(x) = 100 \times \frac{x - \text{min}}{\text{max} - \text{min}} Decreasing direction (lower better, e.g., adverse events): v(x)=100×max−xmax−minv(x) = 100 \times \frac{\text{max} - x}{\text{max} - \text{min}}  use: - Default choice (FDA/EMA recommendation) - strong evidence non-linear preferences - Transparency simplicity priorities - clinical outcomes Advantages: - Simple interpretable - Transparent calculations - Regulatory acceptance - Conservative (neutral) assumptions Disadvantages: - May reflect true stakeholder preferences - Assumes equal marginal value across range

title: MCDA Value Function Types
text: Description: Different slopes different regions, connected breakpoints  use: - Clinical guidelines define severity categories (mild/moderate/severe) - Regulatory thresholds exist (acceptable/concerning/unacceptable) - Stakeholders value ranges differently - MCID (Minimally Clinically Important Difference) creates natural breakpoints Example applications: - QoL scales clinical interpretation bands - Lab values normal/borderline/abnormal ranges - Dose levels safety thresholds Advantages: - Captures clinical interpretation thresholds - flexible linear - Still relatively transparent - Can match stakeholder preferences better Disadvantages: - parameters justify - Arbitrary breakpoint placement needs rationale - complex linear

title: MCDA Value Function Types
text: Description: Curved relationship reflecting risk attitudes Risk-averse (diminishing returns), b > 1: v(x)=100×(x−minmax−min)bv(x) = 100 \times \left(\frac{x - \text{min}}{\text{max} - \text{min}}\right)^b Risk-seeking (increasing returns), 0 < b < 1: v(x)=100×(x−minmax−min)bv(x) = 100 \times \left(\frac{x - \text{min}}{\text{max} - \text{min}}\right)^b  use: - Stakeholder elicitation reveals non-linear preferences - Diminishing returns clinically meaningful (first 50% efficacy valuable) - Severity increases exponentially (rare catastrophic events) - Utility theory supports risk attitudes Example applications: - Mortality (life equally valuable - linear power=1) - Quality life (diminishing returns high levels) - Rare serious AEs (exponential concern) Advantages: - Captures risk attitudes - Single parameter (power) adjust - Smooth differentiable Disadvantages: - Requires justification non-linearity - Power parameter selection subjective - Less transparent linear

title: MCDA Value Function Types
text: Description: Slow change extremes, rapid change middle v(x)=1001+exp(−k×(x−midpoint))v(x) = \frac{100}{1 + \exp(-k \times (x - \text{midpoint}))}  use: - Threshold effects (outcome “tips” clinical cutpoint) - low/high values matter less mid-range - Psychological preferences show S-shaped patterns - Binary clinical decisions gray zone Example applications: - Biomarkers clinical interpretation thresholds - PROs “clinically meaningful change” bands - Dose-response therapeutic window Advantages: - Captures threshold effects - Smooth transition (sharp jumps) - Biologically/clinically realistic many outcomes Disadvantages: - Two parameters (midpoint, steepness) justify - Can complex explain - Requires clear rationale threshold location

title: MCDA Value Function Types
text: Description: Discrete value levels categorical outcomes  use: - Outcomes inherently categorical (CTCAE grades) - Clinical practice uses discrete classifications - meaningful distinction within categories - Regulatory guidance defines categories Example applications: - CTCAE toxicity grades (1-5) - ECOG performance status (0-4) - Disease severity classifications Advantages: - Matches clinical practice - Simple interpretation - Natural categorical data Disadvantages: - Ignores variation within categories - Sharp discontinuities may reflect reality - Can sensitive threshold placement

title: MCDA Value Function Types
text: Let’s visualize function types outcome:  Key observations: - Linear: Constant concern across levels - Piecewise: Different concern different ranges - Exponential: Increasing concern (risk-averse) - Sigmoid: Threshold effect ~25% - Step: Categorical interpretation

title: MCDA Value Function Types
text: Default recommendation: Linear value functions - Transparent conservative - Neutral assumption (risk attitude imposed) - Easiest justify explain non-linear may acceptable: 1. Strong clinical/theoretical rationale 2. Validated stakeholder elicitation 3. Pre-specified analysis plan 4. Sensitivity analysis shows robustness 5. Documented justification

title: MCDA Value Function Types
text: Clinical validity: - function match clinical understanding? - clinical thresholds categories? - clinicians think linearly non-linearly outcome? Stakeholder preferences: - Elicit preferences using standard techniques - Choice experiments - Swing weighting - Direct function specification Data requirements: - Linear: min/max thresholds - Piecewise: Breakpoints slopes - Exponential: Power parameter - Sigmoid: Midpoint steepness - Step: Category thresholds values Interpretability: - Can explain regulators? - Can patients understand ? - make intuitive sense?

what: Compare Linear to Alternative Value Function Types
title: MCDA Value Function Types
text: Create comparison plots showing linear functions compare approaches (piecewise, exponential, sigmoid, step) benefits risks:  visualization helps stakeholders understand: - linear function (current standard, shown gray) provides neutral, transparent approach - alternative functions transform clinical data differently - regulatory agencies prefer linear functions (simplicity, transparency, imposed risk attitudes) - potential impact choosing non-linear approaches benefit-risk conclusions Key observations: - Linear (Current Standard): Equal marginal value across levels - regulatory preference - Piecewise Linear: Different slopes different regions - useful clinical thresholds exist - Exponential: Captures diminishing returns increasing concern - requires stakeholder justification - Sigmoid: Threshold effects smooth transitions - useful binary clinical interpretations - Step: Discrete categories - matches categorical clinical practice (e.g., CTCAE grades) functions help communicate MCDA normalization process stakeholders demonstrate linear value function approach used throughout package.

title: MCDA Clinical Threshold-Based Normalization
text: package provides three MCDA visualization functions implement clinical threshold-based normalization: create_mcda_barplot_comparison(): Shows normalized values Placebo Drug side--side difference create_mcda_walkthrough(): Shows complete MCDA calculation normalized differences weighted scores create_mcda_waterfall(): Shows cumulative contribution criterion total benefit-risk score functions use fixed clinical scales (global scales) rather treatment-relative normalization (local scales), recommended FDA/EMA best practices PROTECT framework.

title: MCDA Clinical Threshold-Based Normalization
text: criterion, apply linear value function based clinical thresholds: “increasing” direction (higher better): “decreasing” direction (lower better): min max clinically meaningful thresholds, observed treatment values.
code: v(x) = 100 * (x - min) / (max - min) v(x) = 100 * (max - x) / (max - min)

title: MCDA Clinical Threshold-Based Normalization
text: Apply value function drug’s actual values → Drug normalized (0-100) Apply value function placebo’s actual values → Placebo normalized (0-100) Normalized difference = Drug normalized - Placebo normalized Positive = Drug performs better Placebo Negative = Drug performs worse Placebo Weighted contribution = Normalized difference × Weight Total benefit-risk score = Sum weighted contributions

title: MCDA Clinical Threshold-Based Normalization
text: criterion requires three parameters: Direction: - "increasing": Higher raw values better (e.g., efficacy) - "decreasing": Lower raw values better (e.g., adverse events) Thresholds based clinical guidelines, Minimum Clinically Important Difference (MCID), regulatory precedents.
code: clinical_scales <- list(   `Criterion Name` = list(     min = 0,                  # Lower threshold     max = 100,                # Upper threshold     direction = "increasing"  # "increasing" or "decreasing"   ) )

title: MCDA Clinical Threshold-Based Normalization
text: Comparison Plot: Shows normalized values difference shows 3 columns criterion: - Normalized Placebo (0-100 scale) - Normalized Drug (0-100 scale) - Normalized Difference (Drug - Placebo) Walkthrough Plot: Shows complete MCDA calculation Waterfall Plot: Shows cumulative contributions
code: barplot_comparison <- create_mcda_barplot_comparison(   data = mcda_data,   study = "Study 1",   benefit_criteria = c("Benefit 1", "Benefit 2", "Benefit 3"),   risk_criteria = c("Risk 1", "Risk 2"),   comparison_drug = "Drug A",   clinical_scales = clinical_scales ) barplot_walkthrough <- create_mcda_walkthrough(   data = mcda_data,   study = "Study 1",   benefit_criteria = c("Benefit 1", "Benefit 2", "Benefit 3"),   risk_criteria = c("Risk 1", "Risk 2"),   comparison_drug = "Drug A",   weights = weights,   clinical_scales = clinical_scales ) # Show all active treatments compared to their study-specific comparators waterfall_all <- create_mcda_waterfall(   data = mcda_data,   comparator_name = "Placebo",   benefit_criteria = c("Benefit 1", "Benefit 2", "Benefit 3"),   risk_criteria = c("Risk 1", "Risk 2"),   weights = weights,   clinical_scales = clinical_scales )  # Or analyze a single study waterfall_study1 <- create_mcda_waterfall(   data = mcda_data,   study = "Study 1",   comparator_name = "Placebo",   benefit_criteria = c("Benefit 1", "Benefit 2", "Benefit 3"),   risk_criteria = c("Risk 1", "Risk 2"),   weights = weights,   clinical_scales = clinical_scales )

title: MCDA Clinical Threshold-Based Normalization
text: plot shows 3 panels representing MCDA calculation steps: Panel 1: Normalized Difference - Shows: (Drug normalized - Placebo normalized) - Positive values = Drug performs better Placebo - Negative values = Drug performs worse Placebo - Scale symmetric around zero Panel 2: Weight - Shows: Relative importance (must sum 100%) - criterion’s contribution final score Panel 3: Benefit-Risk - Shows: Normalized Difference × Weight - Weighted contribution criterion - Total score = Sum contributions - Positive total = Drug better overall benefit-risk profile - Negative total = Placebo better overall benefit-risk profile

title: MCDA Clinical Threshold-Based Normalization
text: waterfall chart shows criteria cumulatively contribute total benefit-risk score: Bar Segments - colored segment represents one criterion’s weighted contribution - Blue segments = Benefits (positive contribution) - Red segments = Risks (negative contribution) - Gray bar = Total score (cumulative sum) Dotted Connector Lines - Connect end one bar start next - Show cumulative progression toward total score Interpretation - Segments extending right = Favorable contributions - Segments extending left = Unfavorable contributions - Total bar shows net benefit-risk balance - Positive total = Drug favored comparator - Negative total = Comparator favored drug showing studies, facet panel displays one active treatment compared study-specific comparator, allowing visual comparison across multiple drugs.

title: MCDA Clinical Threshold-Based Normalization
text: Example: Drug 19% adverse events, Placebo 3% adverse events -32 indicates Drug worse safety Placebo (higher AE rate)
code: # Clinical scale: min=0%, max=50%, direction="decreasing"  # Step 1: Normalize separately Drug_normalized = 100 * (50-19) / 50 = 62 Placebo_normalized = 100 * (50-3) / 50 = 94  # Step 2: Compute difference Normalized_difference = 62 - 94 = -32  # Drug performs worse

title: MCDA Clinical Threshold-Based Normalization
text: Setting Thresholds: - Base clinical guidelines, MCID, regulatory precedents - use observed min/max data - Document rationale Validation: - Check face validity (make clinical sense?) - Perform sensitivity analysis weights - Ensure stakeholder acceptance

code: library(valueJudgementCE) library(cowplot) library(gtable)  # Create both plots stacked_bar_fig <- stacked_barchart(   data = comp_outcome,   chartcolors = colfun()$fig12_colors,   ylabel = "Study Week" )  divergent_stacked_bar_fig <- divergent_stacked_barchart(   data = comp_outcome,   chartcolors = colfun()$fig12_colors,   favcat = c(     "Benefit larger than threshold, with AE",     "Benefit larger than threshold, w/o AE"   ),   unfavcat = c(     "Withdrew",     "Benefit less than threshold, w/o AE",     "Benefit less than threshold, with AE"   ),   ylabel = "Study Week" )  # Extract and combine with shared legend stacked_bar_with_legend <- stacked_bar_fig +    labs(fill = "Outcome") +   theme(legend.position = "top",         legend.justification = "center",         legend.box.just = "center",         legend.title.align = 0.5) +   guides(fill = guide_legend(nrow = 2, title.position = "top", title.hjust = 0.5))  g <- ggplotGrob(stacked_bar_with_legend) legend <- g$grobs[[which(g$layout$name == "guide-box-top")]]  stacked_bar_no_legend <- stacked_bar_fig +   theme(     legend.position = "none",     plot.background = element_rect(color = "black", fill = NA, linewidth = 0.5)   ) divergent_stacked_bar_no_legend <- divergent_stacked_bar_fig +   theme(     legend.position = "none",     plot.background = element_rect(color = "black", fill = NA, linewidth = 0.5)   )  combined_plots <- plot_grid(stacked_bar_no_legend, divergent_stacked_bar_no_legend, ncol = 2) plot_grid(legend, combined_plots, ncol = 1, rel_heights = c(0.2, 1))

what: Create Forest and Dot Plots for Treatment Effects — create_forest_dot_plot
title: Create Forest and Dot Plots for Treatment Effects — create_forest_dot_plot
text: Generates side--side forest dot plots specified outcomes, grouped factor type. Displays treatment effects, confidence intervals, optional clinical thresholds. AXIS REVERSAL: benefit outcomes clinical meaningful difference direction = "less", x-axis automatically reverses (positive values left, negative right) green shading extends towards decreasing x values. handles cases lower values indicate better outcomes (e.g., cholesterol reduction).

title: Create Forest and Dot Plots for Treatment Effects — create_forest_dot_plot
code: create_forest_dot_plot(   data,   outcomes_with_thresholds = NULL,   treatment1 = "Drug A",   treatment2 = "Placebo",   filter_value = "None",   precalculated_stats = FALSE,   forest_upper_limit = NULL,   base_font_size = 9 )

title: Create Forest and Dot Plots for Treatment Effects — create_forest_dot_plot
text: data data frame prepared using prepare_forest_dot_data() matching structure. outcomes_with_thresholds Either NULL (uses available outcomes thresholds), character vector outcome names include (thresholds), named list names outcomes values thresholds. lists, directions default "greater" positive values "less" negative values, can specified list(outcome = list(threshold = 0.1, direction = "greater")). treatment1 Character; label first treatment group (default: "Drug "). treatment2 Character; label second treatment group (default: "Placebo"). filter_value Character; value used filter Filter column (default: "None"). precalculated_stats Logical; TRUE, skips calculation uses provided statistics. forest_upper_limit Numeric; optional upper limit forest plot, adds reference line value provided. base_font_size Numeric; base font size points text elements plot (default: 9).

title: Create Forest and Dot Plots for Treatment Effects — create_forest_dot_plot
text: patchwork object containing combined dot forest plots shared legend.

title: Create Forest and Dot Plots for Treatment Effects — create_forest_dot_plot
code: # First, prepare the data prepared_data <- prepare_forest_dot_data(effects_table)  # Generate the plot using all available outcomes with no thresholds dotforest <- create_forest_dot_plot(prepared_data) if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ ggsave_custom("dotforest.png", imgpath = "./", inplot = dotforest, dpi = 300) } # }  # Use only specific outcomes with no thresholds create_forest_dot_plot(prepared_data,   outcomes_with_thresholds = c("Benefit 1", "Benefit 2") )   # Custom thresholds with automatic direction detection dotforest_4pub <- create_forest_dot_plot(prepared_data,   outcomes_with_thresholds = list(     "Benefit 1" = 0.10,     "Benefit 2" = -20,     "Risk 1" = -0.05,     "Risk 2" = -0.07   ) )  if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ ggsave_custom("inst/img/dotforest.png",   imgpath = "./",   inplot = dotforest_4pub, dpi = 300 ) } # }  # Custom thresholds with explicit directions create_forest_dot_plot(prepared_data,   outcomes_with_thresholds = list(     "Benefit 1" = list(threshold = 0.10, direction = "greater"),     "Risk 1" = list(threshold = -0.05, direction = "less")   ) )   # AXIS REVERSAL: Benefit outcomes with direction "less" # When benefit outcomes have clinical meaningful difference # direction = "less", the x-axis reverses (positive left, negative right) # and green shading # extends towards negative values (decreasing x direction) create_forest_dot_plot(prepared_data,   outcomes_with_thresholds = list(     "Benefit 1" = list(threshold = -0.15, direction = "less"),     "Benefit 2" = list(threshold = -0.10, direction = "less")   ) )   # Example: Lower cholesterol levels are better (benefit with negative # direction) # Treatment difference: Drug A - Placebo = -20 mg/dL (Drug A better) # Clinical threshold: -15 mg/dL with direction "less" (values < -15 are # meaningful) # Result: X-axis reverses, green shading extends towards negative values

title: Create MCDA Benefit-Risk Map — create_mcda_brmap
text: Creates benefit-risk map showing trade-aggregated benefits risks treatment. treatment plotted point x-axis represents total weighted benefit score (0-100 scale) y-axis represents transformed risk score (0-100 scale, calculated 100 + risk_score). Higher better axes: high benefit scores indicate benefits vs comparator, high risk scores indicate better risk profiles (fewer/less severe adverse events) vs comparator. example, risk score -10 (slightly worse placebo) becomes 90 map. Treatments upper-right region offer high benefits low risks. function reuses calculation logic create_mcda_walkthrough ensure consistency.

code: # Load example MCDA data data(mcda_data)  # Define clinical scales clinical_scales <- list(   `Benefit 1` = list(min = 0, max = 1, direction = "increasing"),   `Benefit 2` = list(min = 0, max = 100, direction = "decreasing"),   `Benefit 3` = list(min = 0, max = 100, direction = "increasing"),   `Risk 1` = list(min = 0, max = 0.5, direction = "decreasing"),   `Risk 2` = list(min = 0, max = 0.3, direction = "decreasing") )  # Create benefit-risk map (no title/subtitle by default) brmap_plot <- create_mcda_brmap(   data = mcda_data,   comparator_name = "Placebo",   benefit_criteria = c("Benefit 1", "Benefit 2", "Benefit 3"),   risk_criteria = c("Risk 1", "Risk 2"),   clinical_scales = clinical_scales )  # With title and subtitle brmap_with_titles <- create_mcda_brmap(   data = mcda_data,   comparator_name = "Placebo",   benefit_criteria = c("Benefit 1", "Benefit 2", "Benefit 3"),   risk_criteria = c("Risk 1", "Risk 2"),   clinical_scales = clinical_scales,   show_title = TRUE,   show_subtitle = TRUE )  # With custom weights and colors if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ weights <- c(   `Benefit 1` = 0.30,   `Benefit 2` = 0.20,   `Benefit 3` = 0.10,   `Risk 1` = 0.30,   `Risk 2` = 0.10 )  # Custom colors for treatments custom_colors <- c(   "Drug A" = "#FF6B6B",   "Drug B" = "#4ECDC4",   "Drug C" = "#45B7D1",   "Drug D" = "#96CEB4" )  brmap_custom <- create_mcda_brmap(   data = mcda_data,   benefit_criteria = c("Benefit 1", "Benefit 2", "Benefit 3"),   risk_criteria = c("Risk 1", "Risk 2"),   weights = weights,   clinical_scales = clinical_scales,   fig_colors = custom_colors,   show_frontier = TRUE )  # Show only title without subtitle brmap_title_only <- create_mcda_brmap(   data = mcda_data,   comparator_name = "Placebo",   benefit_criteria = c("Benefit 1", "Benefit 2", "Benefit 3"),   risk_criteria = c("Risk 1", "Risk 2"),   clinical_scales = clinical_scales,   show_title = TRUE,   show_subtitle = FALSE ) } # }

title: Create MCDA Bar Chart: Calculation Walkthrough — create_mcda_walkthrough
code: # Load example MCDA data data(mcda_data)  # View the data structure - each study has comparator and active treatment head(mcda_data) #>     Study Treatment Benefit 1 Benefit 2 Benefit 3 Risk 1 Risk 2 #> 1 Study 1   Placebo      0.05        65         9   0.03  0.002 #> 2 Study 1    Drug A      0.46        20        60   0.19  0.015 #> 3 Study 2   Placebo      0.06        50        15   0.01  0.001 #> 4 Study 2    Drug B      0.20        14        18   0.18  0.010 #> 5 Study 3   Placebo      0.04        57        44   0.05  0.001 #> 6 Study 3    Drug C      0.46        50        45   0.36  0.020 #   Study      Treatment Benefit 1 Benefit 2 Benefit 3 Risk 1 Risk 2 # 1 Study 1    Placebo      0.05        65         9   0.30  0.087 # 2 Study 1    Drug A       0.46        20        60   0.46  0.100  # Define clinical scales clinical_scales <- list(   `Benefit 1` = list(min = 0, max = 1, direction = "increasing"),   `Benefit 2` = list(min = 0, max = 100, direction = "decreasing"),   `Benefit 3` = list(min = 0, max = 100, direction = "increasing"),   `Risk 1` = list(min = 0, max = 0.5, direction = "decreasing"),   `Risk 2` = list(min = 0, max = 0.3, direction = "decreasing") )  # Create walkthrough showing the MCDA calculation steps for Drug B barplot_walk <- create_mcda_walkthrough(   data = mcda_data,   study = "Study 2",   benefit_criteria = c("Benefit 1", "Benefit 2", "Benefit 3"),   risk_criteria = c("Risk 1", "Risk 2"),   comparison_drug = "Drug B",   clinical_scales = clinical_scales )  # With custom weights and clinical scales for Drug A if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ weights <- c(   `Benefit 1` = 0.30,   `Benefit 2` = 0.20,   `Benefit 3` = 0.10,   `Risk 1` = 0.30,   `Risk 2` = 0.10 )  # Define clinical scales based on clinical guidelines, MCID, or # regulatory precedents. These fixed scales ensure stability and # interpretability. # Note: The "direction" field specifies which direction is favorable: #   - "increasing": higher values are better #   - "decreasing": lower values are better clinical_scales <- list(   `Benefit 1` = list(     min = 0, # No benefit (unacceptable)     max = 1, # Maximum expected benefit     direction = "increasing"   ),   `Benefit 2` = list(     min = 0, # Best outcome (no symptoms)     max = 100, # Worst outcome (severe symptoms)     direction = "decreasing" # Lower is better (e.g., symptom severity)   ),   `Benefit 3` = list(     min = 0, # No improvement     max = 100, # Maximum improvement     direction = "increasing"   ),   `Risk 1` = list(     min = 0, # No adverse events (ideal)     max = 0.5, # 50% rate (unacceptable threshold)     direction = "decreasing"   ),   `Risk 2` = list(     min = 0, # No adverse events (ideal)     max = 0.3, # 30% rate (concerning threshold)     direction = "decreasing"   ) )  barplot_walk_a <- create_mcda_walkthrough(   data = mcda_data,   study = "Study 1",   benefit_criteria = c("Benefit 1", "Benefit 2", "Benefit 3"),   risk_criteria = c("Risk 1", "Risk 2"),   comparison_drug = "Drug A",   weights = weights,   clinical_scales = clinical_scales ) ggsave(   "inst/img/barplot_mcda_walkthrough_drug_a.png",   barplot_walk_a,   width = 12,   height = 6,   dpi = 300 ) } # }

title: Trade-off plot — generate_tradeoff_plot
text: data (data.frame) input dataset following variables required columns. Note variables Grouped_Outcome, Statistics, Outcome_Status required generating trade-plot, listed required columns key generating value tree, starting point subsequent benefit-risk assessments. Factor: character vector containing whether outcome "Benefit" "Risk" Grouped_Outcome: character vector containing name grouped outcomes, e.g., Infections Outcome: character vector containing name outcomes, e.g., Herpes viral infections, upper respiratory tract infections Statistics: character vector containing summary statistics outcomes, e.g., %, mean change baseline Type: character vector containing whether outcome "Binary" "Continuous" variable Outcome_Status: character vector containing whether outcome "Identified" "Potential" outcome Filter: character vector containing filter subgroup data, "None" filtre applicable. Example: None; Sex. Category: character vector containing  category filtering subgroup data, "" filter applicable. Example: ; Male, Female. Trt1: character vector containing name active treatments Trt2: character vector containing controlled term "Placebo" Drug_Status: character vector containing whether treatment "Approved" "Test" drug following variables situational columns - Filled specific summary statistic related row outcome (ex. proportion): Rate_Type: numeric vector containing whether AE rate "EventRate" "IncRate". Required risk outcomes displayed exposure-adjusted event rate incidence rate. Prop1: numeric vector containing proportion active treatment. Required binary outcomes displayed proportions; can populated nSub1/N1 nSub1 N1 provided. IncRate1: numeric vector containing exposure-adjusted incidence rate per 100 PYs active treatment. Required risk outcomes displayed exposure-adjusted incidence rates; can populated nSub1/PYAR1*100 nSub1 PYAR1 provided. EventRate1: numeric vector containing exposure-adjusted event rate per 100 PYs active treatment. Required risk outcomes displayed exposure-adjusted event rates; can populated nEvent1/PEY1*100 nEvent1 PEY1 provided. Mean1: numeric vector containing mean active treatment. Required continuous outcomes. Prop2: numeric vector containing proportion comparator treatment. Required binary outcomes displayed proportions; can populated nSub2/N2 nSub1 N1 provided. IncRate2: numeric vector containing exposure-adjusted incidence rate per 100 PYs comparator treatment. Required risk outcomes displayed exposure-adjusted incidence rates; can populated nSub2/PYAR2*100 nSub1 PYAR1 provided. code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Load the data data(mcda_data)  # View structure - note the Study column head(mcda_data)  # Define clinical scales clinical_scales <- list(   `Benefit 1` = list(min = 0, max = 1, direction = "increasing"),   `Benefit 2` = list(min = 0, max = 100, direction = "decreasing"),   `Benefit 3` = list(min = 0, max = 100, direction = "increasing"),   `Risk 1` = list(min = 0, max = 0.5, direction = "decreasing"),   `Risk 2` = list(min = 0, max = 0.3, direction = "decreasing") )  # Analyze a specific study barplot_study1 <- create_mcda_barplot_comparison(   data = mcda_data,   study = "Study 1",   comparison_drug = "Drug A",   benefit_criteria = c("Benefit 1", "Benefit 2", "Benefit 3"),   risk_criteria = c("Risk 1", "Risk 2"),   clinical_scales = clinical_scales )  # Analyze all studies together (if they share a common comparator) waterfall_all <- create_mcda_waterfall(   data = mcda_data,   comparator_name = "Placebo",   benefit_criteria = c("Benefit 1", "Benefit 2", "Benefit 3"),   risk_criteria = c("Risk 1", "Risk 2"),   clinical_scales = clinical_scales ) } # }

code: # Load example MCDA data data(mcda_data)  # View the data structure - each row has raw values for a treatment head(mcda_data) #>     Study Treatment Benefit 1 Benefit 2 Benefit 3 Risk 1 Risk 2 #> 1 Study 1   Placebo      0.05        65         9   0.03  0.002 #> 2 Study 1    Drug A      0.46        20        60   0.19  0.015 #> 3 Study 2   Placebo      0.06        50        15   0.01  0.001 #> 4 Study 2    Drug B      0.20        14        18   0.18  0.010 #> 5 Study 3   Placebo      0.04        57        44   0.05  0.001 #> 6 Study 3    Drug C      0.46        50        45   0.36  0.020 #   Treatment Benefit 1 Benefit 2 Benefit 3 Risk 1 Risk 2 # 1   Placebo      0.05        65         9   0.30  0.087 # 2    Drug A      0.46        20        60   0.46  0.100 # 3    Drug B      ...  # Define clinical scales clinical_scales <- list(   `Benefit 1` = list(min = 0, max = 1, direction = "increasing"),   `Benefit 2` = list(min = 0, max = 100, direction = "decreasing"),   `Benefit 3` = list(min = 0, max = 100, direction = "increasing"),   `Risk 1` = list(min = 0, max = 0.5, direction = "decreasing"),   `Risk 2` = list(min = 0, max = 0.3, direction = "decreasing") )  # Define weights weights <- c(   `Benefit 1` = 0.30,   `Benefit 2` = 0.20,   `Benefit 3` = 0.10,   `Risk 1` = 0.30,   `Risk 2` = 0.10 )  # Create sensitivity plot toggling criterion weight by 20 percent sensitivity_plot <- mcda_tornado(   data = mcda_data |>            dplyr::filter(Study == "Study 1") |>              dplyr::select(-Study),   comparison_drug = "Drug A",   clinical_scales = clinical_scales,   weights = weights )

Must vector length 3, first color corresponding scatter plot points, second corresponding overall mean, third written probability text color. base_font_size Numeric; base font size points text elements plot (default: 9).
